checks.a_analysis: The output JSON faithfully represents the content of the source document without inventing any new text, headings, or sections. The section titles and bullet points match the source exactly.

There is no contact information (phone numbers, emails, addresses) in the source document. The output correctly reflects the placeholder fields (Year, Organization, Form Contact) without adding any fabricated data.
All pages and sections from the source document are present in the JSON output. This includes all checklist items across the four main categories on page 1 and page 2.
The source document contains no tables; the checklist items are presented as lists, which are correctly handled in the output.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: Each section in the JSON contains the full set of checklist items found in the source. No significant sentences or items have been omitted.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the JSON output is a high-fidelity transcription of the source PDF. Even minor typos from the original (e.g., 'milage' instead of 'mileage') are preserved, ensuring no semantic changes were introduced.

checks.a_analysis: I have reviewed both pages of the source PDF and the converted JSON output. The text from the headers, labels, and all checklist items appears exactly once in the output, mirroring its appearance in the source document. No sections, paragraphs, or list items are repeated incorrectly.

The output uses an H2 for the primary header title and organizes the major checklist categories into distinct section titles. This effectively maps the document's structure for screen readers. There are no skipped levels or incorrect nesting identified in the JSON structure.
The source document contains no tables; it consists entirely of headers, form labels, and checkbox lists. The output correctly identifies zero tables.

The reading order in the JSON follows the logical top-to-bottom flow of the document: Header -> Form Labels -> Staffing & Operations Management -> Documentation & Record Keeping -> Technical Support & Infrastructure -> Emergency Operations Coordination. This matches the flow across the two pages of the source PDF.

The checklist items are correctly grouped into unordered lists matching their respective categories in the source. The number of items in each list (3, 3, 2, and 4 respectively) accurately reflects the source document. The checkbox characters are preserved within the list item text.
There are no hyperlink URLs or email addresses present in the source text. The output correctly contains no link elements.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output converts the two-page PDF checklist into a continuous single-column layout. The logical sequence of headers and checklist items is preserved from the source. The relocation of the QR code from the page footer to a dedicated 'Document Header' section at the top of the HTML is a logical organizational change for a web document and does not disrupt the user's ability to navigate the information. The visual flow remains consistent with the source document's intent.
The source PDF does not contain any tables. The document is structured as a series of headings followed by checklist items. Therefore, no table rendering issues occur.

The source PDF contains two prominent organizational logos in the header of both pages: the 'Hurri Up' logo (featuring a hurricane graphic and stylized text) and the 'LGC Local Government Committee' logo. These logos are completely absent from the HTML output. While the QR code image was successfully preserved, the omission of the primary branding logos—especially the 'Hurri Up' logo which contains unique text not found elsewhere in the document—represents a failure to render meaningful visual content from the source.
checks.b_evidence_source: 'Hurri Up' logo and 'LGC Local Government Committee' logo images located in the top header section of Page 1 and Page 2.
checks.b_evidence_output: The images are absent from the HTML rendering; the document begins directly with text headings.

checks.a_analysis: A review of the HTML screenshot shows that no content blocks, paragraphs, or sections from the source are repeated. Each checklist item and organizational header appears exactly once in the sequence.
